relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: David reaches royal rest after years of pursuit and war.
From a cedar palace he notices the ark still sheltered by fabric.
Nathan initially blesses the king's intention without waiting for a new word.
Night interrupts confident religious planning.
YHWH asks whether David is the one who should construct a permanent dwelling.
Divine presence remembers freedom of movement through tent and tabernacle.
No earlier shepherd-leader was commanded to replace mobile presence with cedar.
David is reminded that he came from pasture rather than palace.
Protection and reputation are named as gifts rather than self-manufactured greatness.
The promise imagines Israel planted beyond terror, though every planting story must remember those already on the land.
The word reverses David's plan: YHWH, not David, will build the house.
Dynasty will continue through a descendant after David joins his ancestors.
The future son will build for the Name, and his throne is spoken of as enduring.
Royal sonship includes correction, expressed through the violent discipline language of the ancient world.
Covenant-kindness is promised not to disappear as it did from Saul.
House, kingdom, and throne are gathered into the difficult promise of forever.
Nathan delivers both the refusal and the dynasty promise.
David sits before YHWH and begins from smallness rather than entitlement.
He recognizes that the promise reaches far beyond his own lifetime.
Speech quiets before the One who knows the servant beneath the king.
David locates greatness in Divine purpose rather than royal merit.
Praise names YHWH incomparable without requiring contempt for other peoples.
Israel's redemption story becomes communal identity, though its conquest rhetoric remains ethically charged.
Covenant binds people and Divine through enduring relationship.
David asks the promise concerning his house to become reality.
The desired stability of dynasty is joined to magnifying YHWH's Name.
Revelation gives David courage to pray for what he has heard.
He rests his request upon truth and promise.
The prayer closes by asking that blessing remain upon the royal house forever.

contemplative_note: David wants to build a house for Divine presence; the answer reminds him that presence was already moving freely and that the deeper building is covenant. Any later use of this promise must resist dynasty worship, empire, replacement theology, and the temptation to trap the Holy inside royal architecture.
